audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Hellenists/Hebrews complaint, seven appointed, Stephen, synagogue dispute, false witnesses.

verses.restored: In these days, as the disciples were multiplying, a murmuring arose of the Hellenists against the Hebrews, because their widows were being overlooked in the daily service.
The twelve called the multitude of the disciples and said, It is not pleasing for us to leave the word of the Divine to serve tables.
Therefore, siblings, look for seven men from among you, witnessed as full of Spirit and wisdom, whom we will appoint over this need.
But we will persevere in prayer and in the service of the word.
The word pleased the whole multitude, and they chose Stephen, a man full of trust and holy Spirit, and Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolaus, a proselyte from Antioch.
These they set before the apostles, and praying, they laid hands on them.
The word of the Divine kept growing, and the number of disciples in Jerusalem multiplied greatly; even a great crowd of the priests were obeying the trust.
Stephen, full of grace and power, was doing great wonders and signs among the people.
But some from the synagogue called Freedmen, and Cyrenians, Alexandrians, and those from Cilicia and Asia, rose and disputed with Stephen.
They were not able to withstand the wisdom and the Spirit by which he was speaking.
Then they secretly stirred up men saying, We have heard him speaking blasphemous words against Moses and the Divine.
They stirred up the people, the elders, and the scribes; coming upon him, they seized him and brought him into the council.
They set up false witnesses saying, This human does not cease speaking words against this holy place and the Law.
For we have heard him say that this Jesus the Nazarene will tear down this place and change the customs Moses handed down to us.
Looking intently at him, all those sitting in the council saw his face like the face of a messenger.
